question_html:
Answer the following, giving reasons:

  1. The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of incident radiation.
  2. Photoelectric current increases with the increase in the intensity of the incident radiation.
  3. The stopping potential {tex}{V}_{0}{/tex} varies linearly with the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface.
question_text: Answer the following, giving reasons: The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of incident radiation. Photoelectric current increases with the increase in the intensity of the incident radiation. The stopping potential {tex}{V}_{0}{/tex} varies linearly with the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface.
explanation: With the variation in the intensity of incident radiations, there is a change in the number of photons not on the energies of the photons so the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons remains same. With the increase in intensity of incident radiation number of photons increases as a result photoelectric current increase. With the increase in frequency of incident radiation , the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases. Hence higher value of stopping potential (retarding potential) is required.
solution: With the variation in the intensity of incident radiations, there is a change in the number of photons not on the energies of the photons so the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons remains same. With the increase in intensity of incident radiation number of photons increases as a result photoelectric current increase. With the increase in frequency of incident radiation , the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases. Hence higher value of stopping potential (retarding potential) is required.
solution_html:
1. With the variation in the intensity of incident radiations, there is a change in the number of photons not on the energies of the photons so the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons remains same.
  2. With the increase in intensity of incident radiation number of photons increases as a result photoelectric current increase.
  3. With the increase in frequency of incident radiation , the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases. Hence higher value of stopping potential (retarding potential) is required.

K.E of photoelectron E k = E - {tex}\emptyset_0{/tex} = 4.06 - 2.14 = 1.92 eV

question_html:
Explain the following observations using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

  1. Photoelectric emission does not occur from a surface when the frequency of the light incident on it is less than a certain minimum value.
  2. It is the frequency, and not the intensity of the incident light which affects the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons.
  3. The cut-off voltage ({tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex}) versus frequency ({tex}v{/tex}) of the incident light curve is a straight line with a slope {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex}.
question_text: Explain the following observations using Einstein's photoelectric equation: Photoelectric emission does not occur from a surface when the frequency of the light incident on it is less than a certain minimum value. It is the frequency, and not the intensity of the incident light which affects the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons. The cut-off voltage ( {tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex} ) versus frequency ( {tex}v{/tex} ) of the incident light curve is a straight line with a slope {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex} .
explanation: Einstein Photo electric equation {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} For {tex}v \lt v_{{o}}, K_{\text {max }}{/tex} will be negative Hence, Photoelectric emission is not possible. According to Einstein Photoelectric equation {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex} It shows {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} depends upon frequency only and not depends upon intensity {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex} {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex} This equation represents the equation of straight line ( {tex}y=m x+c{/tex} ) with the slope {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex} .
solution: Einstein Photo electric equation {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} For {tex}v \lt v_{{o}}, K_{\text {max }}{/tex} will be negative Hence, Photoelectric emission is not possible. According to Einstein Photoelectric equation {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex} It shows {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} depends upon frequency only and not depends upon intensity {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex} {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex} This equation represents the equation of straight line ( {tex}y=m x+c{/tex} ) with the slope {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. Einstein Photo electric equation
    {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex}
    {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex}
    For {tex}v \lt v_{{o}}, K_{\text {max }}{/tex} will be negative
    Hence, Photoelectric emission is not possible.
  2. According to Einstein Photoelectric equation
    {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex}
    Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex}
    It shows {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} depends upon frequency only and not depends upon intensity
  3. {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex}
    {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex}
    This equation represents the equation of straight line ({tex}y=m x+c{/tex}) with the slope {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex}.

question_html:
In the study of a photoelectric effect, the graph between the stopping potential V and frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation on two different metals P and Q is shown below.

  1. Which one of the two metals has higher threshold frequency?
  2. Determine the work function of the metal which has greater value.
question_text: In the study of a photoelectric effect, the graph between the stopping potential V and frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation on two different metals P and Q is shown below. Which one of the two metals has higher threshold frequency? Determine the work function of the metal which has greater value.
solution_html:
1. Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by,
    h{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex} + eV
    {tex}V = \frac { h \nu } { e } - \frac { \phi } { e }{/tex}........(i)
    Eq. (1) represents a straight line given by line P and Q, {tex}\frac{\phi}{e}{/tex} represents negative intercept on the Y-axis. Since Q has greater negative intercept, it will have greater {tex}\phi{/tex} (work function) and hence higher threshold frequency.
  2. To know work function of Q, we put V = 0 in (i), 
    {tex}0 = \frac { h \nu } { e } - \frac { \phi } { e } \Rightarrow \phi = h \nu{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 6 \times 10 ^ { 14 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 6.6 \times 6 \times 10 ^ { - 20 } } { 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } \mathrm { eV } = 25 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}

question_html:
1. What is photoelectric effect?
  2. Using the photon picture of light, show how Einstein's photoelectric equation can be established.
  3. What is the photo-electric effect? Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Explain how it enables us to understand the
    1. linear dependence, of the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons, on the frequency of the incident radiation.
    2. existence of a threshold frequency for a given photoemitter.
    3. independence of the maximum energy of emitted photo-electrons from the intensity of incident light.
question_text: What is photoelectric effect? Using the photon picture of light, show how Einstein's photoelectric equation can be established. What is the photo-electric effect? Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Explain how it enables us to understand the linear dependence, of the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons, on the frequency of the incident radiation. existence of a threshold frequency for a given photoemitter. independence of the maximum energy of emitted photo-electrons from the intensity of incident light.
solution_html:
1. Photo-electric effect is the phenomenon of emission of electrons from the surface of metals, when light radiations of suitable frequency fall on them.
  2. When a radiation of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} is incident on a metal surface, it is absorbed in the form of discrete photons each of energy {tex}h\nu{/tex}. Photoelectric emission occurs because of single collision of a photon with a free electron.
    The energy of the photon is used to
    1. free the electron from the metal surface. It is equal to the work function W0 of the metal.
    2. provide kinetic energy to the emitted Electron.
      {tex}\therefore \quad h v=K_{\max }+W_{0}{/tex}
      or {tex}K_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} m v_{\max }^{2}=h v-W_{0}{/tex}
      This is Einstein's photoelectric equation.
    1. Clearly, above the threshold frequency {tex}\nu_0{/tex}, Kmax {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\nu{/tex} i.e., the maximum K.E. of the emitted electrons depends linearly on the frequency of incident radiation.
    2. When v < v0 , Kmax becomes negative. The kinetic energy becomes negative which has no physical meaning. Hence there is no photoelectric emission below the threshold frequency v0.
    3. It is obvious from the photo-electric equation that the maximum K.E. of photo-electrons does not depend on the intensity of incident light.

question_text: Write the basic features of the photon picture of electromagnetic radiation on which Einstein's photoelectric equation is based.
explanation: The basic features of the photon picture of e.m. radiation are as follows: Light is composed of discrete packets of energy called quanta or photons. Each photon carries an energy E ( {tex}=h\nu{/tex} ) and momentum p (= h / {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), which depend on the frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation and not on its intensity. During the collision of a photon with an electron, the total energy of the photon gets absorbed by the electron. Photoelectric emission from the metal surface occurs due to the absorption of a photon by an electron.
solution: The basic features of the photon picture of e.m. radiation are as follows: Light is composed of discrete packets of energy called quanta or photons. Each photon carries an energy E ( {tex}=h\nu{/tex} ) and momentum p (= h / {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), which depend on the frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation and not on its intensity. During the collision of a photon with an electron, the total energy of the photon gets absorbed by the electron. Photoelectric emission from the metal surface occurs due to the absorption of a photon by an electron.
solution_html:
The basic features of the photon picture of e.m. radiation are as follows:

  1. Light is composed of discrete packets of energy called quanta or photons.
  2. Each photon carries an energy E ({tex}=h\nu{/tex}) and momentum p (= h / {tex}\lambda{/tex}), which depend on the frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation and not on its intensity.
  3. During the collision of a photon with an electron, the total energy of the photon gets absorbed by the electron.
  4. Photoelectric emission from the metal surface occurs due to the absorption of a photon by an electron.

When a given photosensitive material is irradiated with light of frequency v, the maximum speed of the emitted photoelectrons equals Vmax. The graph shown in the figure gives a plot of {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\max }^2{/tex} varying with frequency v.

Obtain an expression for

  1. Planck’s constant, and
  2. The work function of the given photosensitive material in terms of the parameters l, n and the mass m of the electron.
  3. How is threshold frequency determined from the plot?
question_text: When a given photosensitive material is irradiated with light of frequency v, the maximum speed of the emitted photoelectrons equals V max . The graph shown in the figure gives a plot of {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\max }^2{/tex} varying with frequency v. Obtain an expression for Planck’s constant, and The work function of the given photosensitive material in terms of the parameters l , n and the mass m of the electron. How is threshold frequency determined from the plot?
explanation: Einstein, Photoelectric equation {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\max }^2=\mathrm{h} v-\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }=\left(\frac{2 h}{m}\right) v-\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}{/tex} Slope of the graph is {tex}\frac{2 h}{m}=\frac{l}{n}{/tex} Planck constant {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{l m}{2 n}{/tex} Intercept on {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }{/tex} , axis, {tex}\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}=l{/tex} Work function {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m l}{2}{/tex} Threshold frequency is the intercept on {tex}\nu{/tex} axis i.e. v 0 = n
solution: Einstein, Photoelectric equation {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\max }^2=\mathrm{h} v-\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }=\left(\frac{2 h}{m}\right) v-\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}{/tex} Slope of the graph is {tex}\frac{2 h}{m}=\frac{l}{n}{/tex} Planck constant {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{l m}{2 n}{/tex} Intercept on {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }{/tex} , axis, {tex}\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}=l{/tex} Work function {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m l}{2}{/tex} Threshold frequency is the intercept on {tex}\nu{/tex} axis i.e. v 0 = n
solution_html:
1. Einstein, Photoelectric equation
    {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\max }^2=\mathrm{h} v-\phi_0{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }=\left(\frac{2 h}{m}\right) v-\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}{/tex}
    Slope of the graph is {tex}\frac{2 h}{m}=\frac{l}{n}{/tex}
    Planck constant {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{l m}{2 n}{/tex}
  2. Intercept on {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }{/tex}, axis, {tex}\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}=l{/tex}
    Work function {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m l}{2}{/tex}
  3. Threshold frequency is the intercept on {tex}\nu{/tex} axis i.e. v0 = n

question_html:
The work function of caesium is 2.14 eV. Find

  1. the threshold frequency for caesium, and
  2. the wavelength of the incident light if the photocurrent is brought to zero by a stopping potential of 0.60 V.
question_text: The work function of caesium is 2.14 eV. Find the threshold frequency for caesium, and the wavelength of the incident light if the photocurrent is brought to zero by a stopping potential of 0.60 V.
solution_html:
1. For the cut-off or threshold frequency, the energy h{tex}\nu_0{/tex} of the incident radiation must be equal to a work function {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex}. So that
    {tex}\nu_{0}=\frac{\phi_{0}}{h}=\frac{2.14 \mathrm{eV}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.14 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s}}=5.16 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{Hz}{/tex}
    Thus, for frequencies less than this threshold frequency, no photoelectrons are ejected.
  2. Photocurrent reduces to zero when the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons equals the potential energy eV0 by the retarding potential V0. Einstein's Photoelectric equation is 
    {tex}e V_{0}=h \nu-\phi_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_{0}{/tex}
    or, {tex}\lambda=h c /\left(e V_{0}+\phi_{0}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}\right)}{(0.60 \times1.6\times10^{-19}+2.14 \times1.6\times10^{-19})}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{19.89 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{Jm}}{(2.74 \mathrm{eV})}{/tex}
    Thus the wavelength is given by:-
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{19.89 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{Jm}}{2.74 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}=454 \mathrm{nm}{/tex}

question_html:
In the study of a photoelectric effect the graph between the stopping potential V and frequency of the incident radiation on two different metals P and Q is shown below.

  1. Which one of the two metals has higher threshold frequency?
  2. Determine the work function of the metal which has greater value.
  3. Find the maximum kinetic energy of electron emitted by light of frequency 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz for this metal.
question_text: In the study of a photoelectric effect the graph between the stopping potential V and frequency of the incident radiation on two different metals P and Q is shown below. Which one of the two metals has higher threshold frequency? Determine the work function of the metal which has greater value. Find the maximum kinetic energy of electron emitted by light of frequency 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz for this metal.
explanation: Q has higher threshold frequency Work function of Q {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = hv {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 2.47 eV KE max = hv - hv 0 = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 ) = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 J = {tex}6.6\times 10^{-{34}} \times2 \times 10^{14} eV\over 1.6\times 10^{-19}{/tex} hence KE max = 0.83 eV
solution: Q has higher threshold frequency Work function of Q {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = hv {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 2.47 eV KE max = hv - hv 0 = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 ) = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 J = {tex}6.6\times 10^{-{34}} \times2 \times 10^{14} eV\over 1.6\times 10^{-19}{/tex} hence KE max = 0.83 eV
solution_html:
1. Q has higher threshold frequency
  2. Work function of Q {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = hv
    {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
    = 2.47 eV
  3. KEmax = hv - hv0
    = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34(8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014)
    = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014J
    = {tex}6.6\times 10^{-{34}} \times2 \times 10^{14} eV\over 1.6\times 10^{-19}{/tex}
    hence KEmax = 0.83 eV

question_html:
What is the de Broglie wavelength of

  1. a bullet of mass 0.040 kg travelling at the speed of 1.0 km/s,
  2. a ball of mass 0.060 kg moving at a speed of 1.0 m/s, and
  3. a dust particle of mass 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 kg drifting with a speed of 2.2 m/s?
question_text: What is the de Broglie wavelength of a bullet of mass 0.040 kg travelling at the speed of 1.0 km/s, a ball of mass 0.060 kg moving at a speed of 1.0 m/s, and a dust particle of mass 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 kg drifting with a speed of 2.2 m/s?
explanation: Mass of the bullet, m = 0.040 kg Velocity with which the bullet is travelling, v = 1 kms -1 = 10 3 ms -1 using the formula of momentum, p = mv {tex}=0.040 \times 10^{3}{/tex} = 40 kg ms -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of bullet is given by :- {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{40}{/tex} {tex}=1.7 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{m}{/tex} Mass of the ball, m = 0.060 kg Velocity with which the balls is moving, v = 1.0 ms -1 Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 0.060 kg ms -1 Therefore, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of the ball is given by {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{0.060}{/tex} {tex}=1.1 \times 10^{-32} m{/tex} Mass of the dust particle, m {tex}=1.0 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} Velocity of the particle, v = 2.2 m/s Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 kg ms -1 Therefore, De-broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2.2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} {tex}=3 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution: Mass of the bullet, m = 0.040 kg Velocity with which the bullet is travelling, v = 1 kms -1 = 10 3 ms -1 using the formula of momentum, p = mv {tex}=0.040 \times 10^{3}{/tex} = 40 kg ms -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of bullet is given by :- {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{40}{/tex} {tex}=1.7 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{m}{/tex} Mass of the ball, m = 0.060 kg Velocity with which the balls is moving, v = 1.0 ms -1 Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 0.060 kg ms -1 Therefore, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of the ball is given by {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{0.060}{/tex} {tex}=1.1 \times 10^{-32} m{/tex} Mass of the dust particle, m {tex}=1.0 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} Velocity of the particle, v = 2.2 m/s Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 kg ms -1 Therefore, De-broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2.2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} {tex}=3 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Mass of the bullet, m = 0.040 kg
    Velocity with which the bullet is travelling, v = 1 kms-1 = 103 ms-1
    using the formula of momentum,
    p = mv
    {tex}=0.040 \times 10^{3}{/tex}
    = 40 kg ms-1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} De-broglie wavelength of bullet is given by :-
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{40}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.7 \times 10^{-35} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
  2. Mass of the ball, m = 0.060 kg
    Velocity with which the balls is moving, v = 1.0 ms-1
    Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 0.060 kg ms -1
    Therefore,
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
    De-broglie wavelength of the ball is given by {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{0.060}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.1 \times 10^{-32} m{/tex}
  3. Mass of the dust particle, m {tex}=1.0 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{kg}{/tex}
    Velocity of the particle, v = 2.2 m/s
    Momentum of the particle, p = mv = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 kg ms-1
    Therefore,
    De-broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2.2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
    {tex}=3 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{m}{/tex}

question_html:
Ultra-violet light of wavelength 200 nm from a source is incident on a metal surface. If the stopping potential is -2.5 V,

  1. Calculate the work function of the metal, and
  2. How would the surface respond to a high intensity red light of wavelength 6328 A produced by a laser?
question_text: Ultra-violet light of wavelength 200 nm from a source is incident on a metal surface. If the stopping potential is -2.5 V, Calculate the work function of the metal, and How would the surface respond to a high intensity red light of wavelength 6328 A produced by a laser?
explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 200 nm, stopping potential = -2.5 V K.E. = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J E = hv =h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex} = 9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Work function = E - eV = (9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 )J = 5.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Wavelength of red light = 6328 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex} E = hv = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6328 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 0.00314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Energy of red light < work function of metal surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} No emission of photoelectrons takes place.
solution: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 200 nm, stopping potential = -2.5 V K.E. = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J E = hv =h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex} = 9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Work function = E - eV = (9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 )J = 5.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Wavelength of red light = 6328 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex} E = hv = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6328 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 0.00314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Energy of red light < work function of metal surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} No emission of photoelectrons takes place.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 200 nm, stopping potential = -2.5 V
    K.E. = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex}1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J = 4 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
    E = hv =h{tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex}
    = 9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
    Work function = E - eV
    = (9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19)J
    = 5.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
  2. Wavelength of red light = 6328 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex}
    E = hv = h{tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6328 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
    = 0.00314 {tex}\times{/tex}10-16 J
    = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J

Energy of red light < work function of metal surface
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No emission of photoelectrons takes place.

question_text: The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted is doubled when the wavelength of light incident on the photosensitive surface changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} . Deduce expressions for the threshold wavelength and work function for the metal surface in terms of {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} .
explanation: Given that Initial kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by = K 1​ Final kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by K 2 = 2K 1 ​ Wavelength of light changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} Let the threshold frequency is {tex}\nu_0{/tex} ​ and work function is {tex}\phi_0{/tex} Now, we know that:- {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + KE {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 1 ...(i) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 2 ...(ii) K 2 = 2K 1 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 ...(iii) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{2hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}2\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 (eq (i) {tex}\times{/tex} 2) {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}=\phi_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} We know work function is given by {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}= \mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}=\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
solution: Given that Initial kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by = K 1​ Final kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by K 2 = 2K 1 ​ Wavelength of light changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} Let the threshold frequency is {tex}\nu_0{/tex} ​ and work function is {tex}\phi_0{/tex} Now, we know that:- {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + KE {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 1 ...(i) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 2 ...(ii) K 2 = 2K 1 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 ...(iii) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{2hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}2\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 (eq (i) {tex}\times{/tex} 2) {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}=\phi_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} We know work function is given by {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}= \mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}=\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that 
Initial kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by = K1​
Final kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by K2 = 2K1​
Wavelength of light changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex}
Let the threshold frequency is {tex}\nu_0{/tex}​ and work function is {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
Now, we know that:-
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + KE 
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K1 ...(i)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K2 ...(ii)
K2 = 2K1 
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + 2K1 ...(iii)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{2hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}2\phi_0{/tex} + 2K1 (eq (i) {tex}\times{/tex} 2)
{tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}=\phi_{0}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} 
We know
work function is given by {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}= \mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}=\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{/tex}

explanation: The three characteristic features which can't be explained by wave theory are: Kinetic energy of emitted electrons are found to be independent of intensity of incident light. Below a certain frequency (threshold) there is no photoemission. Spontaneous emission of photo-electrons. We have drawn a graph between stopping potential (V 0 ) and frequency (V). Thus the given two conditions are satisfied in the slope potential difference and frequency.

question_text: A deutron and an alpha particle are accelerated with the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has: greater value of de-Broglie wavelength, associated with it, and less kinetic energy? Explain. A proton and a deuteron are accelerated through the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has: greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it, and less momentum? Give reasons to justify your answer.
explanation: Using conservation of energy, change in electrostatic energy equals the kinetic energy gained. E = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = qV p = mv = {tex}\sqrt{2 m qV}{/tex} De-brogile wavelength is given by: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} Now, Charge on deuteron = 1e, Charge on alpha particle = 2e and m α = 2 m d For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{\lambda_{d}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{m_{d} q_{d}}}{/tex} = 2 Hence, wavelength of deuteron is more than the wavelength of the alpha particle. Kinetic energy, E = qV For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{E_{d}}{E_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, kinetic energy of deuteron is less than the kinetic energy of alpha particle. From de-Broglie equation, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} As, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex} ........(i) {tex}\lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex} Ratio of wavelengths of electron and proton, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } \right) }{/tex} Ratio of mass of proton and electron, {tex}\frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } = 1836{/tex} (constant) {tex}\frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } = 1{/tex} (Both electron and proton have same charge) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { 1836 \times 1 }{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { e } \approx 42.8 \lambda _ { p }{/tex} nearly Electron have a greater wavelength associated with it than that of proton. {tex}\because \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} (de-Broglie equation) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { h } { \lambda } \Rightarrow p \propto \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } }{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 }{/tex} Momentum of proton is nearly 42.8 times to that of the momentum of the electron. Thus, the electron will have less momentum.

question_html: When a surface is irradiated with light of {tex}\lambda = 4950\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}, a photocurrent appears which vanishes if a retarding potential greater than 0.6 V is applied across the photo tube. When a different source of light is used, it is found that the critical retarding potential is changed to 1.1 V. What is the work function of the surface and the wavelength of the second source? If the photoelectrons (after emission from the source ) are subjected to a magnetic field of 10 tesla, what changes will be observed in the above two retarding potentials?
question_text: When a surface is irradiated with light of {tex}\lambda = 4950\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} , a photocurrent appears which vanishes if a retarding potential greater than 0.6 V is applied across the photo tube. When a different source of light is used, it is found that the critical retarding potential is changed to 1.1 V. What is the work function of the surface and the wavelength of the second source? If the photoelectrons (after emission from the source ) are subjected to a magnetic field of 10 tesla, what changes will be observed in the above two retarding potentials?
explanation: According to Einstein's equation of photo electricity {tex}\frac{1}{2}mv_{\max }^2 = e{V_0} = h\nu - {\nu_0}{/tex} or {tex}e{V_0} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - {\phi _0}{/tex} where {tex}{\phi _0}{/tex} is the work function, {tex}\lambda{/tex} wavelength of incident light and V 0 is the stopping potential. For the first source, {tex}{\lambda _1} = 4950\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} = 4950 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} V 0 = 0.6 V {tex}\therefore 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 0.6{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{495 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} - {\phi _0}{/tex} or {tex}0.96 \times {10^{ - 19}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 19}} - {\phi _0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\phi _0} = 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} .....(i) {tex} = \frac{{3.04 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}eV{/tex} = 1.9 eV Let {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex} be the wavelength of the second source. Given, {tex}V_0' = 1.1V{/tex} Therefore, {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 1.1{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} - 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} [From (i)] or {tex}1.76 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} - 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} = 4.8 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\lambda _2} = \frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{4.8 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}m = 4.125 \times {10^{ - 7}}m{/tex} {tex}= 4125\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} When the ejected photoelectrons are subjected to the action of a magnetic field, no change in retarding potential will be observed. This is because a magnetic field does not alter the kinetic energy of the photoelectrons. The magnetic field only changes the direction of motion.

For the first source, {tex}{\lambda _1} = 4950\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} = 4950 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} V 0 = 0.6 V {tex}\therefore 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 0.6{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{495 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} - {\phi _0}{/tex} or {tex}0.96 \times {10^{ - 19}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 19}} - {\phi _0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\phi _0} = 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} .....(i) {tex} = \frac{{3.04 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}eV{/tex} = 1.9 eV Let {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex} be the wavelength of the second source. Given, {tex}V_0' = 1.1V{/tex} Therefore, {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 1.1{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} - 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} [From (i)] or {tex}1.76 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} - 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} = 4.8 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\lambda _2} = \frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{4.8 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}m = 4.125 \times {10^{ - 7}}m{/tex} {tex}= 4125\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} When the ejected photoelectrons are subjected to the action of a magnetic field, no change in retarding potential will be observed. This is because a magnetic field does not alter the kinetic energy of the photoelectrons. The magnetic field only changes the direction of motion.
solution_html:
According to Einstein's equation of photo electricity
{tex}\frac{1}{2}mv_{\max }^2 = e{V_0} = h\nu - {\nu_0}{/tex}
or {tex}e{V_0} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - {\phi _0}{/tex}
where {tex}{\phi _0}{/tex} is the work function, {tex}\lambda{/tex} wavelength of incident light and V0  is the stopping potential.
For the first source,
{tex}{\lambda _1} = 4950\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} = 4950 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex}
V0 = 0.6 V
{tex}\therefore 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 0.6{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{495 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} - {\phi _0}{/tex}
or {tex}0.96 \times {10^{ - 19}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 19}} - {\phi _0}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {\phi _0} = 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} .....(i)
{tex} = \frac{{3.04 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}eV{/tex}
= 1.9 eV
Let {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex} be the wavelength of the second source.
Given, {tex}V_0' = 1.1V{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 1.1{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} - 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} [From (i)]
or {tex}1.76 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} - 3.04 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} = 4.8 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {\lambda _2} = \frac{{19.8 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{4.8 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}m = 4.125 \times {10^{ - 7}}m{/tex}
{tex}= 4125\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex}
When the ejected photoelectrons are subjected to the action of a magnetic field, no change in retarding potential will be observed. This is because a magnetic field does not alter the kinetic energy of the photoelectrons. The magnetic field only changes the direction of motion.

question_html:
Consider a 20 W bulb emitting light of wavelength 5000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} and shining on a metal surface kept at a distance 2m. Assume that the metal surface has a work function of 2 eV and that each atom on the metal surface can be treated as a circular disk of radius 1.5 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}.

  1. Estimated no. of photons emitted by the bulb per second. [Assume no other losses]
  2. Will there be photoelectric emission?
  3. How much time would be required by the atomic disk to receive energy equal to work function (2 eV)?
  4. How many photons would atomic disk receive within the time duration calculated in (c) above?
  5. Can you explain how the photoelectric effect was observed instantaneously?

[Hint: Time calculated in part (c) is from classical consideration and you may further take the target of surface area say 1cm2 and estimate what would happen?]
question_text: Consider a 20 W bulb emitting light of wavelength 5000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} and shining on a metal surface kept at a distance 2m. Assume that the metal surface has a work function of 2 eV and that each atom on the metal surface can be treated as a circular disk of radius 1.5 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} . Estimated no. of photons emitted by the bulb per second. [Assume no other losses] Will there be photoelectric emission? How much time would be required by the atomic disk to receive energy equal to work function (2 eV)? How many photons would atomic disk receive within the time duration calculated in (c) above? Can you explain how the photoelectric effect was observed instantaneously? [Hint: Time calculated in part (c) is from classical consideration and you may further take the target of surface area say 1cm 2 and estimate what would happen?]
explanation: According to the given problem , power emitted is given by P = 20W, wavelength is given by, {tex}\lambda = 5000\mathop A\limits^o = 5000 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} , d = 2m, Work function {tex}\phi_{0}=2 e V{/tex} , {tex}r = 1.5\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} {tex}= 1.5 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex} No. of the photon emitted by bulb per second is {tex}n^{\prime}=\frac{P}{(h c / \lambda)}=\frac{P \lambda}{h c}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{20 \times\left(5000 \times 10^{-10}\right)}{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}=5 \times 10^{19} s^{-1}{/tex} The energy of the incident photon {tex}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}{5000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}=2.48 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} As this energy is greater than 2 eV (i.e., the work function of the metal surface), hence photoelectric emission takes place. Let {tex}\Delta t{/tex} be the time spent in getting the energy {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} (= work function of metal) {tex}\frac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi r^{2} \Delta t=\phi_{0}{/tex} or {tex}\Delta t=\frac{4 \phi_{0} d^{2}}{P r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times 2^{2}}{20 \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2}}=11.4 s{/tex} No. of photons received by an atomic disc in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is {tex}N=\frac{n^{\prime} \times \pi r^{2}}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \Delta t=\frac{n^{\prime} r^{2} \Delta t}{4 d^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(5 \times 10^{19}\right) \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2} \times 11.4}{4 \times(2)^{2}}=0.80 \approx 1{/tex} In photoelectric emission, there is an elastic collision between the incident photon and free electron of the metal surface, which lasts for very very short interval of time ( {tex}\approx 10^{-9}{/tex} ), hence we say photoelectric emission is instantaneous.

Let {tex}\Delta t{/tex} be the time spent in getting the energy {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} (= work function of metal) {tex}\frac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi r^{2} \Delta t=\phi_{0}{/tex} or {tex}\Delta t=\frac{4 \phi_{0} d^{2}}{P r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times 2^{2}}{20 \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2}}=11.4 s{/tex} No. of photons received by an atomic disc in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is {tex}N=\frac{n^{\prime} \times \pi r^{2}}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \Delta t=\frac{n^{\prime} r^{2} \Delta t}{4 d^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(5 \times 10^{19}\right) \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2} \times 11.4}{4 \times(2)^{2}}=0.80 \approx 1{/tex} In photoelectric emission, there is an elastic collision between the incident photon and free electron of the metal surface, which lasts for very very short interval of time ( {tex}\approx 10^{-9}{/tex} ), hence we say photoelectric emission is instantaneous.
solution_html:
According to the given problem , power emitted is given by P = 20W, wavelength is given by,  {tex}\lambda = 5000\mathop A\limits^o = 5000 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex}, d = 2m, Work function  {tex}\phi_{0}=2 e V{/tex}, {tex}r = 1.5\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} {tex}= 1.5 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex}

  1. No. of the photon emitted by bulb per second is
    {tex}n^{\prime}=\frac{P}{(h c / \lambda)}=\frac{P \lambda}{h c}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{20 \times\left(5000 \times 10^{-10}\right)}{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}=5 \times 10^{19} s^{-1}{/tex}
  2. The energy of the incident photon {tex}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}{5000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}=2.48 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
    As this energy is greater than 2 eV (i.e., the work function of the metal surface), hence photoelectric emission takes place.
  3. Let {tex}\Delta t{/tex} be the time spent in getting the energy {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} (= work function of metal)
    {tex}\frac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi r^{2} \Delta t=\phi_{0}{/tex}
    or {tex}\Delta t=\frac{4 \phi_{0} d^{2}}{P r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times 2^{2}}{20 \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2}}=11.4 s{/tex}
  4. No. of photons received by an atomic disc in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is
    {tex}N=\frac{n^{\prime} \times \pi r^{2}}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \Delta t=\frac{n^{\prime} r^{2} \Delta t}{4 d^{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{\left(5 \times 10^{19}\right) \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2} \times 11.4}{4 \times(2)^{2}}=0.80 \approx 1{/tex}
  5.  In photoelectric emission, there is an elastic collision between the incident photon and free electron of the metal surface, which lasts for very very short interval of time ({tex}\approx 10^{-9}{/tex}), hence we say photoelectric emission is instantaneous.
